title: Cleaning and sterilising milking equipment
subtitle: The routine that keeps milk fresh and bacteria out.
excerpt: Milking equipment that is rinsed and sterilised immediately after every milking produces milk with a low bacterial count that keeps for three to four days. Equipment that dries with residue produces high-count milk within the first batch. The routine takes five minutes; neglecting it compounds with every subsequent milking.
sourceNotes: Food Standards Agency, raw milk production guidance, food.gov.uk. Dairy UK, milk hygiene best practice guidelines. British Goat Society, milking and hygiene advice, britishgoatsociety.com.

glossaryTerms.definition: A laboratory measure of bacteria per millilitre of milk. UK raw milk for sale must be below 20,000 colony-forming units per ml at the point of collection. High TBC indicates equipment cleaning failure, mastitis, or both.

content.text: Milk is a near-perfect bacterial growth medium. From the moment it leaves the teat it is being colonised by bacteria from every surface it contacts, the teat skin, the milking pail, the pouring jug, the storage bottle, the hands. Minimising that contamination at source is the whole job of milking hygiene.
Rinse with cold water immediately after milking. Cold water prevents fat from binding to the pail surface; hot water on a pail with milk residue bakes fat on.

Wash with hot water and a dedicated dairy detergent or a strong food-grade sanitiser solution. Never use ordinary household washing-up liquid, it leaves residues that taint milk and fails to remove milk biofilm. Work a brush into all corners of the pail and any joins in fittings.

Sterilise with a dairy sterilising solution (sodium hypochlorite or iodophor-based). Follow the label dilution exactly. Fill the pail and all equipment with the solution, leave for 60 to 90 seconds, then invert to drain without rinsing.
Air dry inverted on a clean draining rack. Equipment stored right-side up traps air moisture and supports bacterial growth.

Rinse sterilised equipment with cold water before use to remove residual sanitiser taste. Check that the pail has no visible residue on the inside surface, a faint grey or brown ring around the waterline indicates biofilm build-up and the pail needs re-cleaning. Wash hands thoroughly with soap before milking and dry on a clean towel, not on work clothing.

Strip the first three to four squirts from each teat into a strip cup (a dark-lidded container that shows flakes or clots) before milking into the collection pail. Abnormal milk (stringy, clotted, watery, or off-colour) indicates subclinical mastitis in that quarter. Milk from a mastitic quarter must be discarded; treat the animal and recheck in 48 hours. The
of milk rises dramatically if mastitic milk is mixed into the batch.

Stainless steel pails and equipment sterilise completely and last indefinitely. Plastic equipment scratches with use, and those scratches harbour biofilm that cannot be removed by standard sanitising. Replace plastic milking equipment when visible scratch marks appear, or use only stainless from the start.
